section: General danger signs (child 2 months up to 5 years)
content: In any sick child aged 2 months up to 5 years, check for four general danger signs: the child is not able to drink or breastfeed, the child vomits everything, the child has had convulsions during this illness, and the child is lethargic or unconscious. The presence of ANY general danger sign classifies the child as having a very severe disease requiring urgent referral. Give the first dose of an appropriate antibiotic, treat hypoglycaemia, and refer URGENTLY to hospital.

section: Cough or difficult breathing — fast breathing thresholds
content: Count the child's breaths in one minute to assess for pneumonia. Fast breathing is defined as 50 breaths per minute or more in an infant aged 2 to 12 months, and 40 breaths per minute or more in a child aged 12 months up to 5 years. A child with cough or difficult breathing plus fast breathing (and no chest indrawing or danger sign) is classified as PNEUMONIA and should receive oral amoxicillin per protocol and be reassessed in 3 days.

Chest indrawing is present when the lower chest wall moves IN as the child breathes in (it must be clearly visible and present at all times). A child with cough or difficult breathing plus chest indrawing, OR plus any general danger sign, OR with stridor in a calm child, is classified as SEVERE PNEUMONIA OR VERY SEVERE DISEASE. Give the first dose of an appropriate antibiotic and refer URGENTLY to hospital.

Classify dehydration in a child with diarrhoea using four signs: general condition (lethargic/unconscious or restless/irritable), sunken eyes, the child's reaction when offered fluid (not able to drink or drinking eagerly/thirsty), and skin pinch (goes back very slowly ≥2 seconds, or slowly). SEVERE DEHYDRATION is two or more of: lethargic/unconscious, sunken eyes, not able to drink or drinking poorly, skin pinch goes back very slowly. SEVERE DEHYDRATION requires urgent IV rehydration (Plan C) and referral; SOME DEHYDRATION is managed with ORS under observation (Plan B).

Assess acute malnutrition by mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) and bilateral pitting oedema. A MUAC less than 11.5 cm (115 mm) in a child 6-59 months, OR visible severe wasting, OR bilateral pitting oedema of both feet, classifies SEVERE ACUTE MALNUTRITION. Check for medical complications and appetite; complicated cases or those failing the appetite test need inpatient referral, while uncomplicated cases enter community management with ready-to-use therapeutic food.

section: Young infant (0 up to 2 months) — possible serious bacterial infection
content: In a young infant aged up to 2 months, signs of POSSIBLE SERIOUS BACTERIAL INFECTION or VERY SEVERE DISEASE include: not feeding well, convulsions, fast breathing (60 breaths per minute or more), severe chest indrawing, fever (38°C or above) or low body temperature (below 35.5°C), movement only when stimulated or no movement at all, and umbilical redness extending to the skin. Any one sign requires the first dose of intramuscular antibiotics and URGENT referral to hospital.

Suspect measles in a child with fever plus a generalized maculopapular rash and one of cough, runny nose, or red eyes. Classify SEVERE COMPLICATED MEASLES if there is any general danger sign, clouding of the cornea, or deep/extensive mouth ulcers; give vitamin A, first-dose antibiotic, treat the eyes/mouth, and refer urgently. Give vitamin A for measles in all cases. Watch for measles complications including pneumonia, diarrhoea, and corneal involvement.

section: Danger signs in pregnancy requiring immediate referral
content: Every pregnant woman must be screened for danger signs at each ANC contact. Refer immediately for any of: vaginal bleeding, severe headache, blurred vision, convulsions or fits, high fever, severe abdominal pain, fast or difficult breathing, reduced or absent fetal movements, leaking of fluid before term, and severe vomiting. Any single danger sign mandates urgent transfer to a facility capable of emergency obstetric care (FRU/CEmOC). Arrange referral transport (102/108) without delay.

section: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy — BP thresholds
content: Check blood pressure and test urine for protein at every ANC visit after 20 weeks gestation. Gestational hypertension is systolic BP ≥140 mmHg or diastolic ≥90 mmHg on two occasions; pre-eclampsia is this BP plus proteinuria. SEVERE pre-eclampsia is BP ≥160/110 mmHg and/or signs such as severe headache, visual disturbance, epigastric/right upper quadrant pain, or hyperreflexia. Severe pre-eclampsia and eclampsia (convulsions) are obstetric emergencies: give magnesium sulphate per protocol, control BP, and refer urgently.

Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) is blood loss of 500 mL or more after vaginal birth, or any blood loss causing haemodynamic instability. Suspect PPH with heavy or continuous vaginal bleeding after delivery, passage of large clots, a soft (atonic) uterus, or signs of shock such as fast weak pulse, low BP, pallor, and dizziness. PPH is a leading cause of maternal death: call uterine massage, give a uterotonic per protocol, secure IV access, and refer urgently to an FRU.

section: Anaemia in pregnancy — severity and action
content: Screen every pregnant woman for anaemia by haemoglobin estimation. Mild anaemia is Hb 10.0-10.9 g/dL, moderate is 7.0-9.9 g/dL, and severe is below 7.0 g/dL. Severe anaemia, or any anaemia with breathlessness at rest, tachycardia, or signs of cardiac failure, requires urgent referral for investigation and possible transfusion. Moderate anaemia is treated with therapeutic iron-folic acid and re-checked; all pregnant women receive prophylactic IFA.

Suspect malaria in any patient with acute fever, classically cyclical fever with chills and rigors followed by sweating, especially in endemic areas. Confirm with a rapid diagnostic test (RDT) and/or microscopy BEFORE treatment whenever feasible; do not treat on clinical grounds alone if testing is available. Treat confirmed cases by species (ACT for P. falciparum, chloroquine plus primaquine per protocol for P. vivax). Refer SEVERE malaria — impaired consciousness, convulsions, severe anaemia, jaundice, dark urine, respiratory distress, or hypotension — urgently for parenteral artesunate.

In suspected dengue (acute high fever with headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgia, rash), watch for WARNING SIGNS that mark progression to severe dengue, typically around defervescence (days 3-7): severe persistent abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, clinical fluid accumulation, mucosal bleeding, lethargy or restlessness, liver enlargement, and a rising haematocrit with rapid platelet fall. Any warning sign requires admission and close monitoring. Severe dengue (shock, severe bleeding, organ impairment) is a medical emergency requiring urgent referral and fluid resuscitation.

Suspect enteric (typhoid) fever in sustained, stepwise fever of 4-7 days or more with headache, abdominal discomfort, anorexia, relative bradycardia, and sometimes constipation or diarrhoea; coated tongue and hepatosplenomegaly may be present. Confirm with blood culture where available (Widal alone is unreliable). Treat with an appropriate antibiotic per local susceptibility. Refer for complications such as intestinal perforation (sudden severe abdominal pain, peritonitis), significant GI bleeding, or altered sensorium.

section: Approach to acute undifferentiated febrile illness
content: For acute undifferentiated fever in rural settings, take a focused history (duration, pattern, travel/exposure, localizing symptoms) and look for danger signs of severe disease: altered consciousness, hypotension, bleeding, jaundice, breathlessness, oliguria, and persistent vomiting. Test for malaria (RDT/smear) first; consider dengue, typhoid, scrub typhus, and leptospirosis based on local epidemiology. Fever 7 days or more, or any danger sign, warrants escalation/referral and avoidance of empirical steroids. Do not delay referral while awaiting results in a deteriorating patient.

Suspect acute coronary syndrome (ACS) with chest pain or heaviness lasting more than a few minutes, often central/retrosternal, sometimes radiating to the left arm, jaw, or back, associated with sweating, breathlessness, nausea, or a sense of doom. This is a time-critical emergency. Give aspirin (chew 300 mg) unless contraindicated, keep the patient at rest, and refer/transfer IMMEDIATELY to a facility with ECG and acute cardiac care. Obtain an ECG within 10 minutes where available.

Acute coronary syndrome may present ATYPICALLY in women, people with diabetes, and the elderly. Warning features include epigastric burning or indigestion-like discomfort, unexplained breathlessness, profuse sweating (diaphoresis), nausea or vomiting, fatigue, or syncope WITHOUT classic chest pain. A high index of suspicion is essential because these presentations are easily missed and carry high mortality. Treat as ACS until proven otherwise: give aspirin if not contraindicated, and refer immediately for ECG and cardiac evaluation.

Recognize acute stroke using BE-FAST: Balance (sudden loss of balance/coordination), Eyes (sudden visual loss or double vision), Face (facial drooping), Arm (arm or leg weakness, usually one side), Speech (slurred or difficult speech), and Time (note the time of onset and act fast). Any new focal deficit of sudden onset is a stroke until proven otherwise and is time-critical. Refer/transfer IMMEDIATELY to a facility with CT and stroke care; record the last-known-well time because thrombolysis is time-bound.

Hypertension in non-pregnant adults is a sustained systolic BP ≥140 mmHg and/or diastolic ≥90 mmHg on repeated measurement. A BP ≥180/110 mmHg is severe; if accompanied by symptoms of target-organ damage (chest pain, breathlessness, severe headache, visual disturbance, focal neurological deficit, or reduced urine output) it is a hypertensive emergency requiring urgent referral. Otherwise initiate/uptitrate antihypertensive therapy and lifestyle measures and arrange follow-up. Always exclude stroke and ACS when BP is very high with symptoms.

Consider presumptive pulmonary tuberculosis in anyone with cough lasting 2 weeks or more, OR any duration of cough with fever, unexplained weight loss, night sweats, or haemoptysis (coughing blood). Such individuals should undergo sputum testing with a WHO-recommended rapid molecular test (NAAT, e.g. CBNAAT/TrueNat) as the initial diagnostic. Haemoptysis warrants prompt evaluation and, if massive, urgent referral. Confirmed cases are notified and started on standard anti-TB treatment under NTEP/DOTS.

Haemoptysis (coughing up blood or blood-stained sputum) is a red-flag symptom that requires evaluation for tuberculosis, bronchiectasis, malignancy, and pneumonia. Distinguish from haematemesis and epistaxis. Any haemoptysis warrants sputum NAAT for TB and chest imaging. MASSIVE haemoptysis (large-volume or causing breathlessness/airway compromise) is a medical emergency: position the patient with the bleeding side down if known, secure the airway, and refer urgently.

Assess acute asthma/wheeze severity by ability to speak, respiratory rate, use of accessory muscles, and oxygen saturation. SEVERE features include inability to complete sentences in one breath, respiratory rate ≥30/min, pulse ≥120/min, and SpO2 below 92%. LIFE-THREATENING features include silent chest, cyanosis, exhaustion, confusion, or SpO2 below 90%. Give inhaled short-acting beta-agonist (salbutamol) by spacer/nebuliser and oxygen; severe or life-threatening exacerbations need systemic steroids and urgent referral.

Suspect sepsis when a patient with suspected or confirmed infection deteriorates. Use bedside qSOFA: respiratory rate ≥22/min, altered mentation (any new confusion/drowsiness), and systolic BP ≤100 mmHg; two or more suggest high risk. Other warning signs include fever or hypothermia, tachycardia, mottled skin, reduced urine output, and SpO2 below 92%. Sepsis is a time-critical emergency: take cultures if feasible, give the first dose of broad-spectrum antibiotics early, start IV fluids for hypotension, and refer urgently.

section: Atypical sepsis in the elderly — delirium as a presentation
content: In elderly patients, sepsis frequently presents WITHOUT fever. New-onset confusion or delirium, increased drowsiness, falls, reduced oral intake, weakness, or functional decline may be the only signs of a serious infection (commonly urinary tract infection or pneumonia). Do not exclude sepsis on the basis of a normal temperature. Treat new delirium in an older adult as possible sepsis until proven otherwise: assess vitals, screen for infection source, and escalate to at least an urgent in-person review.

Suspect urinary tract infection (UTI) with dysuria, frequency, urgency, and suprapubic discomfort; cloudy or foul-smelling urine supports it. Uncomplicated lower UTI in non-pregnant adults is treated with a short antibiotic course per local protocol. Features of UPPER tract/complicated infection (pyelonephritis) — high fever with chills, flank/loin pain, vomiting, or sepsis signs — and UTI in pregnancy, men, children, or the elderly require closer evaluation and possible referral. New confusion in an elderly patient may be the only sign.

Treat every snakebite as potentially venomous and DO NOT DELAY transfer. Look for neurotoxic envenomation (ptosis, drooping eyelids, blurred/double vision, difficulty swallowing or breathing, limb weakness) and haemotoxic envenomation (persistent bleeding from the bite or gums, spontaneous bleeding, swelling spreading up the limb, passing dark/red urine). A bedside 20-minute whole blood clotting test (20WBCT) that fails to clot indicates coagulopathy. Immobilise the limb, avoid tourniquets/incisions, and transport immediately to the nearest facility stocking anti-snake venom (ASV).

section: Animal bite — rabies post-exposure prophylaxis categories
content: Classify animal-bite (dog/cat/monkey) exposures for rabies PEP. Category I (touching/feeding, licks on intact skin) needs no PEP. Category II (minor scratches/abrasions without bleeding, nibbling of uncovered skin) needs immediate wound washing and rabies vaccine. Category III (single or multiple transdermal bites or scratches, licks on broken skin, mucous-membrane contact, or any bat exposure) requires immediate wound washing with soap and water for 15 minutes, rabies vaccine, AND rabies immunoglobulin infiltrated around the wound. Start PEP without delay; rabies is almost always fatal once symptomatic.

section: Active suicidal ideation — signposting and escalation
content: Treat any disclosure of active suicidal ideation, a plan, intent, or a recent attempt as a mental-health emergency. Do not leave the person alone; remove access to means where possible and ensure a responsible adult stays with them. This is signposting and supportive contact, NOT treatment or counselling by the triage system. Escalate for urgent in-person mental-health assessment and provide the national mental-health helpline Tele-MANAS 14416 (available 24x7). For a child or adolescent, also involve Childline 1098.

Suspect an acute surgical abdomen with severe, persistent, or worsening abdominal pain, especially with guarding, rigidity, rebound tenderness, a board-like abdomen, absent bowel sounds, persistent vomiting, fever, or abdominal distension. These suggest peritonitis from perforation, appendicitis, obstruction, or ectopic pregnancy (in a woman of reproductive age, always exclude ectopic). Keep the patient nil by mouth, give IV fluids, do NOT give strong analgesia that masks signs before assessment, and refer urgently for surgical evaluation.

Suspect diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) in a person with diabetes (or new hyperglycaemia) who has excessive thirst and urination, vomiting, abdominal pain, deep rapid breathing (Kussmaul) with an acetone (fruity) breath odour, dehydration, drowsiness, or confusion. Capillary blood glucose is typically very high (often >250 mg/dL) with ketones present. DKA is a metabolic emergency: secure IV access, begin fluid resuscitation, and refer urgently for insulin therapy and electrolyte management. Look for a precipitant such as infection or missed insulin.

Suspect hypoglycaemia in any person on diabetes treatment (especially insulin or sulphonylureas) with sweating, tremor, palpitations, hunger, confusion, slurred speech, abnormal behaviour, seizures, or loss of consciousness; blood glucose is usually below 70 mg/dL. Hypoglycaemia can mimic stroke or intoxication. If the patient is conscious, give 15-20 g of fast-acting oral glucose/sugar and recheck; if unconscious or unable to swallow, do NOT give anything by mouth, place in recovery position, and refer urgently for IV dextrose. Sulphonylurea-induced hypoglycaemia can be prolonged and needs observation.

During an active convulsion, protect the patient from injury, place them on their side (recovery position), do not restrain or put anything in the mouth, and time the seizure. A seizure lasting 5 minutes or more, or repeated seizures without recovery of consciousness between them, is STATUS EPILEPTICUS and a medical emergency needing a benzodiazepine and urgent referral. Always check and correct hypoglycaemia, and in pregnancy treat convulsions as eclampsia (magnesium sulphate). A first-ever seizure, or seizure with fever/head injury/focal deficit, warrants evaluation and referral.

Classify the duration and type of a child's diarrhoea in addition to dehydration. Diarrhoea lasting 14 days or more is PERSISTENT DIARRHOEA; with dehydration it is SEVERE PERSISTENT DIARRHOEA needing referral, otherwise manage nutrition and micronutrients and follow up. Blood in the stool is DYSENTERY: treat with an appropriate antibiotic for Shigella per protocol and zinc, and follow up in 3 days. Give zinc and continued feeding for all childhood diarrhoea, plus ORS for fluid replacement.

Advise every pregnant woman in the third trimester to monitor fetal movements and report any reduction or absence promptly. A perceived decrease in fetal movements, or no movements felt over several hours, may indicate fetal compromise and requires urgent assessment of fetal wellbeing (fetal heart rate, and where available a non-stress test or ultrasound). Do not reassure and discharge without evaluation. Reduced fetal movement is one of the recognised pregnancy danger signs warranting same-day facility review.

An uncomplicated upper respiratory tract infection (common cold) typically presents with runny or blocked nose, sneezing, sore throat, mild cough, and low-grade fever, and is self-limiting over about a week. Manage symptomatically with fluids, rest, and antipyretics; antibiotics are NOT indicated for viral URI. Advise return/escalation if there is high or persistent fever beyond 3-5 days, fast or difficult breathing, chest pain, ear pain, or symptoms worsening rather than improving. In children, always re-screen for IMCI fast-breathing thresholds before labelling a cough as minor.

Assess burns by extent (percentage of total body surface area), depth, and site. Stop the burning process and cool the burn with running water for about 20 minutes; do NOT apply ice, oils, or toothpaste. Refer urgently for burns involving the face, hands, feet, perineum, or major joints, circumferential burns, inhalation injury (facial burns, soot in nostrils, hoarse voice, stridor), electrical or chemical burns, and any large-area burn. Provide analgesia, cover the burn with a clean dressing, and begin fluids for extensive burns while arranging transfer.
guideline_version: WHO Emergency Care / Basic Emergency Care 2018

section: Trauma — primary survey and danger signs
content: In any significant trauma, use the ABCDE primary survey: Airway (with cervical-spine protection), Breathing, Circulation (control external haemorrhage with direct pressure), Disability (consciousness, pupils, glucose), and Exposure. Red flags requiring urgent transfer include reduced consciousness, uncontrolled or heavy bleeding, signs of shock (fast weak pulse, pallor, cold extremities, low BP), penetrating injury to the chest/abdomen/head, suspected spinal injury, and an open or deformed fracture. Immobilise suspected spinal injuries, keep the patient warm, and refer immediately.
guideline_version: WHO Emergency Care / Basic Emergency Care 2018
